title: Mesur y Gymraeg (Cymru) 2011 a Safonau'r Gymraeg / Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011 and Welsh Language Standards
short: Mesur y Gymraeg a'r Safonau / Welsh Language Measure and Standards
chapters: This file covers TWO interlinked instruments as an integrated system: (1) Mesur y Gymraeg (Cymru) 2011 / Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011 (mwa/2011/1) — the primary legislation establishing the Welsh language's official status, creating the Welsh Language Commissioner (Comisiynydd y Gymraeg), and providing the framework for Welsh language standards. 156 sections in 10 Parts + 13 Schedules. (2) Rheoliadau Safonau'r Gymraeg / Welsh Language Standards Regulations — a series of statutory instruments (No. 1 (2015, wsi/2015/996), No. 2 (2016), No. 4 (2016), No. 5 (2017), No. 6 (2017), No. 7 (2018), amended 2025) specifying 176 individual standards across 5 categories. The Measure creates the FRAMEWORK. The Standards provide the OPERATIONAL DETAIL. Together they form the most comprehensive statutory Welsh language protection regime in history.
bilingualNote: Mae'r Mesur a'r Safonau ar gael yn Gymraeg ac yn Saesneg. Y Gymraeg sydd â statws swyddogol yng Nghymru — nid cyfieithiad yw'r testun Cymraeg ond testun cyd-wreiddiol cyfartal. / The Measure and Standards are available in Welsh and English. Welsh has official status in Wales — the Welsh text is a co-equal original, not a translation. Eileen should use bilingual terminology when advising clients who operate in Wales or provide services to Welsh speakers.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Mesur y Gymraeg (Cymru) / Welsh Language (Wales) Measure
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Alun Ffred Jones AM, Heritage Minister, Welsh Government, 25 March 2010
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementDate: Various — commenced in stages from 2011, Standards from 2015
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: The Measure was enacted under the National Assembly for Wales's Measure-making powers (Government of Wales Act 2006). It replaced the Welsh Language Act 1993 framework. The Standards Regulations have been issued in 7 sets (No. 1 to No. 7) covering different categories of organisations, most recently amended by the Welsh Language Standards (Amendment) Regulations 2025 (wsi/2025/339, in force 28 March 2025) adding the Welsh Revenue Authority and other bodies.

significantAmendments.instrument: Welsh Language Standards (No. 1, No. 2, No. 4, No. 6 and No. 7) Regulations (Amendment) Regulations 2025 (wsi/2025/339)
significantAmendments.effect: Added the Welsh Revenue Authority and other bodies to the list of organisations subject to compliance notices. In force 28 March 2025.

precedingDocuments.title: Deddf yr Iaith Gymraeg 1993 / Welsh Language Act 1993
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor legislation. Established the Welsh Language Board and Welsh language schemes. ABOLISHED by the 2011 Measure and replaced with the Commissioner and Standards framework.

precedingDocuments.title: Cymraeg 2050: Miliwn o Siaradwyr Cymraeg / A Million Welsh Speakers
precedingDocuments.significance: The Welsh Government's vision for 1 million Welsh speakers by 2050. The Standards regime is a key delivery mechanism for this target.

parts.title: Rhan 1 / Part 1 — Y Mesur: Statws Swyddogol, y Comisiynydd, a'r Fframwaith Safonau / The Measure: Official Status, the Commissioner, and the Standards Framework

sections.title: Beth mae'r Mesur yn ei wneud / What the Measure does — official status, the Commissioner's powers, and the compliance framework
sections.summary: The Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011 is the constitutional foundation for the Welsh language in public life. It declares Welsh an official language of Wales, establishes the Welsh Language Commissioner with investigation and enforcement powers, creates the framework for Welsh language standards, and establishes the Welsh Language Tribunal. It replaces the Welsh Language Act 1993 framework with a stronger, rights-based system.
sections.text:
STATWS SWYDDOGOL / OFFICIAL STATUS (s.1):

The Measure confirms the Welsh language has OFFICIAL STATUS in Wales. This means:
(a) The Welsh language must NOT be treated less favourably than the English language (y Gymraeg i beidio â chael ei thrin yn llai ffafriol na'r Saesneg).
(b) Persons in Wales must be able to live their lives through the medium of Welsh if they choose.

This is the constitutional principle underpinning the entire Standards regime. Every obligation flows from this principle.

COMISIYNYDD Y GYMRAEG / WELSH LANGUAGE COMMISSIONER (Part 2, ss.2-16):

The Measure establishes the office of Welsh Language Commissioner — an independent statutory office holder who:

(a) PROMOTES and FACILITATES the use of the Welsh language.
(b) Works towards ensuring that the Welsh language is treated no less favourably than English.
(c) INVESTIGATES complaints about failures to comply with Welsh language standards.
(d) Conducts INQUIRIES into matters relating to the Welsh language.
(e) Issues COMPLIANCE NOTICES requiring bodies to comply with specified standards.
(f) Takes ENFORCEMENT ACTION for non-compliance — including investigation, determination, and the imposition of remedies.
(g) Can impose CIVIL PENALTIES (fines) for failure to comply with enforcement actions.
(h) Prepares a 5-YEAR REPORT on the position of the Welsh language.

Current Commissioner: Efa Gruffudd Jones (appointed 2022, succeeding Aled Roberts who served 2019-2022, who in turn succeeded Meri Huws, the inaugural Commissioner 2012-2019).

The Commissioner has TEETH — unlike the Future Generations Commissioner (who relies on soft power), the Welsh Language Commissioner can investigate, determine non-compliance, and impose enforcement actions including civil penalties. This is the strongest enforcement mechanism in the Welsh devolved framework.

Y FFRAMWAITH SAFONAU / THE STANDARDS FRAMEWORK (Part 4, ss.25-46):

The Measure creates the legal framework for Welsh language standards:

(a) Welsh Ministers SPECIFY standards by regulations (the Standards Regulations).
(b) The Commissioner decides WHICH standards apply to WHICH organisations by serving COMPLIANCE NOTICES.
(c) Compliance notices are tailored — not every body must comply with every standard. The Commissioner determines what is reasonable and proportionate for each body.
(d) Standards are grouped into 5 CATEGORIES:
    — Safonau Cyflenwi Gwasanaethau / SERVICE DELIVERY STANDARDS
    — Safonau Llunio Polisi / POLICY MAKING STANDARDS
    — Safonau Gweithredol / OPERATIONAL STANDARDS
    — Safonau Hybu / PROMOTION STANDARDS
    — Safonau Cadw Cofnodion / RECORD KEEPING STANDARDS

(e) A body must COMPLY with any standard specified in its compliance notice by the day stated in the notice.
(f) FAILURE to comply: the Commissioner can investigate, determine non-compliance, take enforcement action, and impose civil penalties.

TRIBIWNLYS Y GYMRAEG / WELSH LANGUAGE TRIBUNAL (Part 6, ss.120-127):

A tribunal established to hear appeals against enforcement actions by the Commissioner. A body that receives an enforcement action can appeal to the Tribunal, which can confirm, vary, or revoke the Commissioner's decision.

RHYDDID I DDEFNYDDIO'R GYMRAEG / FREEDOM TO USE WELSH (Part 5, ss.108-119):

The Measure makes it unlawful to INTERFERE with an individual's freedom to use Welsh with another person who has consented to its use. The Commissioner can investigate complaints about interference with the freedom to use Welsh.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Measure is the constitutional foundation. It does two things: (1) declares Welsh an official language that must not be treated less favourably than English, and (2) creates the institutional framework (Commissioner, standards, compliance notices, enforcement, tribunal) to make that principle operational. The Commissioner has genuine enforcement powers — investigation, compliance notices, enforcement actions, and civil penalties. This is not aspirational — it is LEGALLY BINDING.
refs: Comisiynydd y Gymraeg / Welsh Language Commissioner — Efa Gruffudd Jones (2022-present)
Tribiwnlys y Gymraeg / Welsh Language Tribunal
commonErrors: Not knowing that the Welsh Language Commissioner has ENFORCEMENT POWERS — unlike the Future Generations Commissioner who relies on soft power, the Welsh Language Commissioner can investigate, determine non-compliance, and impose civil penalties. This is the strongest enforcement mechanism in the Welsh devolved framework

Assuming Welsh language obligations are 'nice to have' — they are LEGALLY BINDING. A compliance notice from the Commissioner has the force of law. Non-compliance can result in enforcement action and civil penalties

Not understanding that compliance notices are TAILORED — the Commissioner determines which standards apply to each body based on what is reasonable and proportionate. Two bodies in the same sector may have different obligations

Conflating the old Welsh Language Scheme system with the new Standards system — the Welsh Language Act 1993 framework (language schemes) was abolished by the 2011 Measure and replaced with the Standards framework. If your organisation still has a Welsh Language Scheme, it should have been superseded by a compliance notice

versions.text: IN FORCE: the Measure has been fully commenced since 2015 (when the Standards Regulations began). The institutional framework (Commissioner, Tribunal, compliance notices) is fully operational. Most recently amended by the Welsh Language Standards (Amendment) Regulations 2025 (adding new bodies to the regime from 28 March 2025).
versions.policyRationale: Replacing the discretionary Welsh Language Scheme system with a rights-based framework of legally binding standards enforceable by an independent Commissioner.

parts.title: Rhan 2 / Part 2 — Y Safonau: Y 176 o Safonau mewn 5 Categori / The Standards: 176 Standards in 5 Categories

sections.title: Beth mae'r Safonau yn ei olygu i gyflogwyr / What the Standards mean for employers — the 5 categories in practical detail
sections.summary: The Standards Regulations specify 176 individual standards grouped into 5 categories. Each category addresses a different dimension of Welsh language use. For an HR leader, the Operational Standards are the most directly relevant — they cover recruitment, workforce skills, grievances in Welsh, and training. But ALL categories have employment implications. The Commissioner issues tailored compliance notices specifying which standards apply to each organisation.
sections.text:
Y PUM CATEGORI / THE FIVE CATEGORIES:

═══ CATEGORI 1 — SAFONAU CYFLENWI GWASANAETHAU / SERVICE DELIVERY STANDARDS ═══

These standards govern how an organisation provides services to the public. They are the most visible standards — they determine what a member of the public can expect when interacting with a Welsh public body.

Key requirements:
(a) GOHEBIAETH / CORRESPONDENCE: If a person writes to you in Welsh, you must reply in Welsh. If you initiate correspondence, you must do so bilingually or in the person's preferred language.
(b) FFÔN / TELEPHONE: You must answer calls with a bilingual greeting. You must provide a Welsh language telephone service (not just an 'option to transfer'). You must not make a caller wait longer because they want to speak in Welsh.
(c) CYFARFODYDD / MEETINGS: If a member of the public wishes to use Welsh in a meeting with you, you must facilitate this (e.g., through a bilingual meeting or simultaneous translation).
(d) DOGFENNAU / DOCUMENTS: All documents, forms, and publications aimed at the public must be available bilingually. Welsh versions must be available at the same time as English versions — not 'following translation'.
(e) GWEFAN / WEBSITE: Your website must be fully available in Welsh. Welsh pages must contain the same information as English pages.
(f) ARWYDDION / SIGNAGE: All signs must be bilingual, with Welsh appearing no less prominently than English. Welsh text should appear first (above or to the left of English text).
(g) CYFRYNGAU CYMDEITHASOL / SOCIAL MEDIA: Posts must be bilingual or available in both languages.
(h) DIGWYDDIADAU / EVENTS: Public events must offer Welsh language participation.

═══ CATEGORI 2 — SAFONAU LLUNIO POLISI / POLICY MAKING STANDARDS ═══

These standards require organisations to consider the IMPACT on the Welsh language when making policy decisions.

(a) When formulating a new policy, or revising an existing policy, you must consider: what effects the policy decision would have on opportunities for persons to use the Welsh language; how the policy could be formulated (or changed) so that it would have positive effects, or increased positive effects; and how the policy could be formulated (or changed) so that it would not have adverse effects, or decreased adverse effects.
(b) You must publish a statement of the analysis conducted.

Employment relevance: when restructuring, reorganising, or changing service delivery, you must consider whether the change will reduce Welsh language services or opportunities for staff to use Welsh.

═══ CATEGORI 3 — SAFONAU GWEITHREDOL / OPERATIONAL STANDARDS ═══

These are the standards most DIRECTLY relevant to HR leaders. They govern how the organisation operates INTERNALLY in relation to the Welsh language.

(a) RECRIWTIO / RECRUITMENT:
— You must assess the Welsh language skills needed for every post.
— Where Welsh language skills are desirable or essential, this must be stated in the job advertisement.
— Application forms and interview processes must be available in Welsh.
— Interview panels must include Welsh language assessment capability where Welsh is desirable or essential.
— The organisation must develop a Welsh language skills strategy — assessing current skills, identifying gaps, and planning how to develop Welsh language capability.

(b) POLISI IAITH FEWNOL / INTERNAL LANGUAGE POLICY:
— The organisation must offer staff the right to use Welsh with each other and in internal communications.
— Internal signage must be bilingual.
— Staff intranet must be available in Welsh.
— HR policies (grievance, disciplinary, well-being, complaints) must be available in Welsh.
— Staff must be able to raise a GRIEVANCE IN WELSH and have it handled in Welsh if they wish.

(c) HYFFORDDIANT / TRAINING:
— Training opportunities must be available in Welsh where reasonably practicable.
— Welsh language training must be offered to staff who wish to develop their Welsh skills.
— Mandatory training materials should be available bilingually.

(d) IAITH GWAITH / WORKING LANGUAGE:
— Staff who can speak Welsh should be identified (e.g., 'Iaith Gwaith' badges/lanyards showing they are Welsh-speaking).
— Staff directories should indicate Welsh language capability.
— Welsh-speaking staff should be encouraged to use Welsh in the workplace.

═══ CATEGORI 4 — SAFONAU HYBU / PROMOTION STANDARDS ═══

These standards require organisations to take steps to PROMOTE and INCREASE the use of the Welsh language.

(a) Prepare and publish a 5-year PROMOTION STRATEGY.
(b) The strategy must include: targets for increasing Welsh language use in the organisation, activities to support the Welsh language in the community, and measures to promote the Welsh language to young people.
(c) Report annually on progress.

Applies primarily to local authorities and national park authorities.

═══ CATEGORI 5 — SAFONAU CADW COFNODION / RECORD KEEPING STANDARDS ═══

These standards require organisations to keep records of their compliance with the other standards.

(a) Record the number and nature of complaints received about Welsh language services.
(b) Record the number of staff who have Welsh language skills, by level.
(c) Record the Welsh language skills assessments conducted for new and vacant posts.
(d) Publish an ANNUAL REPORT on compliance with the standards.

IMPACT ON PRIVATE SECTOR EMPLOYERS:

The Standards currently apply primarily to PUBLIC BODIES. Private companies are not directly subject to compliance notices (with limited exceptions for regulated sectors). BUT:

(a) CONTRACTING WITH WELSH PUBLIC BODIES: If you provide services to a Welsh public body under contract, the public body's compliance notice may require IT to ensure that its contractors provide bilingual services. This obligation flows DOWN THE SUPPLY CHAIN through contract terms.

(b) REGULATED SECTORS: Standards Regulations No. 5 (2017) and No. 7 (2018) extend standards to some private and third sector bodies including water companies, energy suppliers, post offices, rail operators, and telecoms companies operating in Wales.

(c) THE COMMISSIONER'S AMBITION: The Commissioner's 2026 Manifesto proposes extending standards to further UK Government departments operating in Wales (HMRC, DWP, DVLA) and to additional private sector bodies. Monitor for expansion of the regime.

(d) PRACTICAL REALITY: Even if the Standards do not directly apply to your company, demonstrating Welsh language capability gives you a competitive advantage when bidding for Welsh public contracts, particularly in areas with high Welsh-speaking populations (Gwynedd, Ynys Môn, Ceredigion, Carmarthenshire).

THE COMPLIANCE NOTICE PROCESS:

(1) Welsh Ministers specify standards in regulations (Standards Regulations).
(2) The Commissioner conducts a STANDARDS INVESTIGATION — assessing which standards should apply to a body.
(3) The Commissioner serves a COMPLIANCE NOTICE on the body — specifying which standards it must comply with and by when.
(4) The body must COMPLY with the standards by the specified date.
(5) If the body fails to comply, the Commissioner can INVESTIGATE.
(6) If non-compliance is determined, the Commissioner can take ENFORCEMENT ACTION:
    — Require the body to take specific steps to comply.
    — Require the body to prepare an action plan.
    — Publicise the non-compliance.
    — Impose a CIVIL PENALTY (fine).
(7) The body can APPEAL to the Welsh Language Tribunal.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Standards are LEGALLY BINDING requirements, not aspirational guidelines. They are tailored to each organisation through compliance notices. For HR leaders, the Operational Standards directly affect recruitment (Welsh language skills assessment for every post), internal communications (right to use Welsh, grievances in Welsh), and training (Welsh language development). For private sector employers contracting with Welsh public bodies, the Standards flow down the supply chain through contract terms.
refs: Welsh Language Standards (No. 5) Regulations 2017 — extending to regulated sectors
Welsh Language Standards (No. 7) Regulations 2018 — health sector

regulatoryGuidance.publisher: Comisiynydd y Gymraeg / Welsh Language Commissioner
regulatoryGuidance.title: Commissioner's guidance and compliance notices
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The Commissioner's website contains all compliance notices, guidance documents, annual reports, and investigation outcomes.

commonErrors: Not knowing that every POST in a listed public body must have its Welsh language requirements assessed — the Operational Standards require a Welsh language skills assessment for EVERY new and vacant post. This is not optional — the Commissioner monitors compliance

Assuming that staff cannot raise a grievance in Welsh — the Operational Standards give staff the right to use Welsh in internal processes, including grievances. If an employee wants their grievance handled in Welsh, the employer must facilitate this

Not providing bilingual recruitment materials — job advertisements, application forms, and interview processes must be available in Welsh for listed public bodies. Failure to do so may deter Welsh-speaking applicants and breach the Standards

Not identifying Welsh-speaking staff — the Iaith Gwaith ('Working Welsh') scheme encourages organisations to identify Welsh-speaking staff through badges and lanyards. This helps the public identify who they can speak Welsh with and helps the organisation deploy Welsh language skills effectively

Assuming the Standards will never apply to your private company — the regime is EXPANDING. The Commissioner's 2026 Manifesto proposes extending standards to more private and UK Government bodies. Even now, contracting with Welsh public bodies may flow Welsh language obligations to you through contract terms

Not planning a Welsh language workforce strategy — listed bodies must assess current Welsh language skills across the workforce, identify gaps, and plan how to develop capability. This is a strategic HR planning obligation, not a box-ticking exercise

Treating Welsh language requirements as a barrier rather than an opportunity — Welsh language skills are in increasing demand. Developing Welsh language capability in your workforce strengthens your competitive position for Welsh public contracts and demonstrates commitment to Welsh communities

pendingAmendments.status: Added Welsh Revenue Authority and other bodies from 28 March 2025. Monitor for further extensions to additional bodies.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — expanding the scope of bodies subject to compliance notices

pendingAmendments.instrument: Commissioner's 2026 Manifesto — proposed extension to UK Government departments
pendingAmendments.status: The Commissioner proposes extending standards to HMRC, DWP, DVLA, DBS, and other UK Government bodies operating in Wales. If implemented, this would significantly expand the scope of Welsh language obligations.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — would extend Welsh language obligations to UK Government services in Wales

pendingAmendments.instrument: Cymraeg 2050 — progress towards 1 million speakers target
pendingAmendments.status: Census 2021 showed 538,296 Welsh speakers — a decrease of 23,000 since 2011. Bold interventions required to reach 1 million by 2050. The Standards regime is a key delivery mechanism.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — may drive stronger standards requirements and enforcement

pendingAmendments.instrument: SPPP Act interaction — Welsh language as part of fair work and procurement
pendingAmendments.status: Welsh language capability is part of the 'Vibrant Culture and Thriving Welsh Language' well-being goal. Procurement decisions should consider Welsh language requirements. Monitor for explicit inclusion in social public workforce clauses.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — Welsh language and procurement increasingly linked

surveillanceStatus.status: IN FORCE — mature framework (Standards operational since 2015), most recently amended March 2025
